title: Honest Abe's Special Day
story: Every February 12, Americans celebrate the birthday of Abraham Lincoln, the 16th President of the United States! Schools often close for the day, and many people visit Lincoln memorials or museums. Kids learn about his famous tall hat, his log cabin childhood, and how he helped end slavery. title: Darwin Day: Celebrating Science and Discovery
story: Scientists and nature lovers around the world celebrate Darwin Day every February 12 to honor Charles Darwin, the famous scientist who discovered how animals and plants change over time. Schools host special science lessons about evolution, museums put on exhibits about fossils and wildlife, and kids get excited about learning how giraffes got their long necks or why penguins can't fly. story: Every February 12, people celebrate National Lost Penny Day by hunting for those sneaky copper coins that disappear into couch cushions, car seats, and jacket pockets! On this quirky holiday, families turn penny-hunting into a fun game, checking under furniture, in washing machines, and between car seats. story: Every February 12, people around the world hold up red handprints to say no to using children as soldiers. People also wear red gloves, share red hand photos online, and sign petitions to protect children in dangerous places. title: Nature's Fury: A Powerful Tornado Strikes
story: A fierce wind roared through Alabama in 1945. On February 12, a massive tornado tore across York and Livingston. This wasn't just any twister - it was rated F4 on the Fujita scale, nearly the strongest possible! The storm's power was terrifying, but it also sparked new interest in tornado safety. Today, we're much better at predicting these dangerous storms and keeping people safe.
activity_question: What was the tornado rated on the Fujita scale?
follow_up_question: How do you think meteorologists predict tornadoes today?

title: Fierce Tornado Strikes Alabama
story: A powerful tornado tore through Montgomery, Alabama in 1945. Whirling winds reached F3 intensity on the Fujita scale, causing widespread damage. The twister destroyed about 100 houses, two warehouses, and even a freight train. Despite the destruction, the community came together to rebuild. Today, improved warning systems help keep people safer when storms approach.
activity_question: What was destroyed by the tornado besides houses and warehouses?
follow_up_question: How do meteorologists measure tornado strength today?

title: Ringo's All-Star Jam Session
story: Rock legend Ringo Starr assembled a supergroup of musical talents in 1999. On February 12, his fifth All-Starr Band took the stage for the first time. This star-studded lineup featured Todd Rundgren, Jack Bruce, Gary Brooker, Simon Kirke, and Timmy Cappello. Together, they created an unforgettable night of music, blending their unique styles and hit songs.
activity_question: Who was the leader of the All-Starr Band?
bonus_fact: Ringo Starr was the drummer for the famous band The Beatles.

title: A Champion for Women's Rights
story: Hendrik Marchant, a Dutch hero for equality, was born in Deventer, Netherlands on February 12, 1869. He grew up to become an important politician who fought for women's right to vote. Marchant's hard work paid off when his special bill helped give women the power to have a say in their country's future. Thanks to people like him, women in the Netherlands gained the right to vote.
activity_question: What did Hendrik Marchant's special bill help achieve?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important for everyone to have the right to vote?

title: Rebel Radio Rocks the Airwaves
story: Microphones crackled to life as Pirate Radio Free Harlem hit the airwaves on February 12, 1967. This underground station in New York City boldly broadcast without a license, giving voice to the community. DJs spun tunes and shared local news, creating a buzz of excitement. While authorities frowned upon pirate radio, it became a powerful tool for expression and connection in Harlem.
activity_question: What type of radio station began broadcasting in 1967?
follow_up_question: Why do you think people started pirate radio stations?

title: Breaking Barriers in Army Nursing
story: Nancy Leftenant made history in 1948. She became the first African American to join the Army Nurse Corps. Leftenant's brave step forward opened doors for others. Her achievement happened on February 12, paving the way for more diversity in military healthcare. Leftenant's dedication to serving her country and helping others inspired many who followed in her footsteps.
activity_question: What did Nancy Leftenant become the first African American to do?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important to have diversity in healthcare roles?

title: From Cleveland to LA: The Rams' Journey
story: Touchdown! The Cleveland Rams joined the NFL huddle in 1937. On February 12 that year, they were granted a franchise, kicking off their football journey. The team later moved and became the Los Angeles Rams we know today. Their story shows how sports teams can change cities while keeping their spirit alive.
activity_question: What city did the Rams originally come from?
bonus_fact: The Rams have won the Super Bowl three times in their history.

title: Nebraska's Bone-Chilling Deep Freeze
story: Brrr! Imagine a cold so intense it could freeze your breath! On February 12, 1899, Camp Clarke, Nebraska experienced just that. The temperature plummeted to an unbelievable -47°F (-44°C), setting the state record for the coldest day ever. Residents bundled up in layers, and even the toughest cowboys shivered in their boots!
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Camp Clarke, Nebraska?
follow_up_question: How do you think people stayed warm during such extreme cold?

title: Airship Adventure: The USS Macon's Last Flight
story: Soaring through stormy skies, the USS Macon faced its final journey on February 12, 1935. This massive airship, as big as three football fields, battled fierce winds off the California coast. Despite the danger, brave crew members worked together, helping 64 people reach safety. While two lives were sadly lost, the Macon's story reminds us of the importance of teamwork in tough times.
activity_question: How many people were saved from the USS Macon?
follow_up_question: Why do you think airships like the USS Macon aren't commonly used for travel today?

title: Satellite Makes a Splash in Space
story: Splash! The Soviet Union launched a special satellite into orbit. Kosmos 1076 blasted off on February 12, 1979. This wasn't just any satellite - it was the first Soviet spacecraft designed to study Earth's oceans from space. Like a high-flying fish, it swam through the stars, peering down at our blue planet's waters.
activity_question: What was special about Kosmos 1076?
activity_choices: It was the first Soviet oceanographic satellite
It was the fastest satellite ever launched
It was shaped like a fish
It could swim in the ocean
activity_answer: It was the first Soviet oceanographic satellite
follow_up_question: Why do you think studying oceans from space might be helpful?

title: Vasco da Gama's Epic Second Journey
story: Brave explorer Vasco da Gama set off on another amazing adventure on February 12, 1502. He sailed from Lisbon, Portugal, heading for India once again. This time, he led a fleet of ships across vast oceans, facing storms and unknown dangers. Da Gama's journey opened up new trade routes and connected different parts of the world.
activity_question: Where did Vasco da Gama sail from on his second voyage to India?
follow_up_question: Why do you think explorers like Vasco da Gama were important in history?

title: A Grand Tribute to Lincoln Begins
story: Crowds gathered in Washington, D.C. for an exciting event in 1914. On February 12, a special ceremony marked the start of building the Lincoln Memorial. This massive monument would honor Abraham Lincoln, one of America's greatest presidents. People watched as officials laid the first stone, imagining the grand tribute that would soon rise from the ground.
activity_question: What was the purpose of the ceremony in Washington, D.C.?
activity_choices: To mark the start of building the Lincoln Memorial
To plant a giant sequoia tree
activity_answer: To mark the start of building the Lincoln Memorial
bonus_fact: The Lincoln Memorial took eight years to complete after this ceremony.

title: Christian Dior's Fashion Revolution
story: Swirling skirts and nipped waists took Paris by storm! On February 12, 1947, French designer Christian Dior unveiled his first collection. It was so fresh and exciting that it earned the nickname 'New Look'. Dior's designs brought elegance back to fashion after World War II, with feminine silhouettes that celebrated curves and luxury.
activity_question: What nickname did Christian Dior's first collection earn?
bonus_fact: Dior's 'New Look' used about 20 yards of fabric per outfit, which was considered very luxurious at the time.
